# sourcevision

> Codebase analysis generated by Sourcevision.
> When using this document: group related items together and separate unrelated concerns.

## Project Overview

- **Files**: 141
- **Lines of code**: 45,901
- **Languages**: TypeScript (128), JSON (7), Markdown (3), Other (3)
- **Zones**: 9 (9 with descriptions)
- **Git**: main @ 63d150a
- **Analyzed**: 2026-03-07T18:58:52.762Z

## Findings

### Anti-patterns

- Global finding 0 (bidirectional coupling analyzers↔unit, warning) is directly contradicted by global finding 9 (LLM analysis). Finding 9 establishes that the 27-crossing unit→analyzers direction is expected test-to-implementation coupling that must not be flagged as an anti-pattern, and that only the 7-crossing analyzers→unit direction represents genuine risk — which is already captured with full specificity by global finding 5. Global finding 0 is superseded by findings 5 and 9 and should be treated as resolved by those two more precise entries. [info]
- God function: registerMcpTools in src/cli/mcp.ts calls 36 unique functions — consider decomposing into smaller, focused functions [warning]

### Patterns

- Zone finding 1 (coupling warning 0.52, 15 imports to analyzers) is a Louvain partitioning artifact corroborated by global finding 10. The 15 crossings are intra-domain edges within one coherent analyzers domain that the algorithm split across zone boundaries. No refactoring of coupling is warranted; the actionable fix is structural (subdirectory organization of src/analyzers/). [info] (analyzers-2)
- Zone finding 0 (coupling warning to analyzers-2) is confirmed as a false positive by two independent sources: zone finding 1 (LLM) and global finding 10 (heuristic cross-check). E2e tests importing from the analyzers domain is correct by design. Severity should be treated as info. [info] (e2e)
- Zone finding 0 (warning: coupling 0.57 to 'analyzers-2') is contradicted by global finding 10. E2e tests coupling to what is semantically one analyzers domain is expected; the warning severity is not justified. The coupling flag measures intra-domain edges that Louvain partitioned into separate zone identifiers, not genuine cross-concern dependencies. [info] (e2e)
- Global finding 0 (bidirectional coupling analyzers ↔ unit, 7+27 crossings) conflates two structurally different relationships. The 27-crossing unit→analyzers direction is expected test-to-implementation coupling (corroborated by unit finding 2 and global finding 9) and carries no risk. The 7-crossing analyzers→unit direction is the sole surviving concern and is captured more precisely by global finding 10. The aggregate bidirectional framing of finding 0 is misleading; only the minority direction (analyzers→unit) warrants a warning, and only pending the audit prescribed by global finding 10. [info]
- The two potentially unused exports — detectBranchLifecycle in src/analyzers/branch-work-filter.ts (global finding 7) and resolveWorkedEpicTitlesForRange in src/cli/commands/prd-epic-resolver.ts (global finding 8) — are independent, low-cost verification tasks. Neither requires architectural analysis: a project-wide grep for each symbol confirms or refutes the dead-export claim in under a minute. These should be verified and the exports removed if unused before any structural reorganization, to avoid carrying dead surface area into the reorganized layout. [info]
- analyzers-2 finding 0 and analyzers-3 finding 0 (generic zone names 'Analyzers 2' and 'Analyzers 3') are symptoms of the same root cause as their coupling/cohesion warnings: Louvain over-partitioning of the src/analyzers/ domain. Generic names are not an enrichment failure—they correctly signal that the algorithm could not identify a distinct semantic identity because none exists independently. After subdirectory reorganization, these artificial zones will collapse into the parent analyzers zone and the naming issue disappears without any manual enrichment. [info]

### Observations

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected [info] (.claude)
- 16 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports [warning] (analyzers)
- Generic zone name "Analyzers 2" — enrichment did not assign a meaningful name reflecting this zone's domain purpose [warning] (analyzers-2)
- High coupling (0.52) — 15 imports target "analyzers" [warning] (analyzers-2)
- Generic zone name "Analyzers 3" — enrichment did not assign a meaningful name reflecting this zone's domain purpose [warning] (analyzers-3)
- High coupling (0.69) — 10 imports target "analyzers" [warning] (analyzers-3)
- Low cohesion (0.31) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone [warning] (analyzers-3)
- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected [info] (e2e)
- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected [info] (fixtures)
- Bidirectional coupling: "analyzers" ↔ "unit" (7+27 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface [warning]
- ... and 5 more

### Suggestions

- Zone "analyzers" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory [info] (analyzers)
- Zone "cli" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory [info] (cli)
- registerMcpTools in src/cli/mcp.ts (36 outgoing calls) should be decomposed into focused sub-registrar functions grouped by capability domain (e.g. registration of analysis tools, status tools, and workflow tools as separate named functions called in sequence). This is an in-file decomposition of the single oversized function — not a file split — which reduces call-graph fan-out and isolates the impact of future tool additions without changing the module's public API. [warning] (cli)
- registerMcpTools in src/cli/mcp.ts is the highest-priority actionable finding in this package, corroborated independently by automated fan-out analysis (36 outgoing calls, global finding 11) and LLM analysis (cli finding 1). Decompose into three sequential named sub-registrar functions within the same file — one per capability domain (analysis tools, status/reporting tools, workflow tools) — called in sequence from registerMcpTools. This is an in-file decomposition only; no file split is required or warranted at this threshold. [warning] (cli)
- Zone "fixtures" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory [info] (fixtures)
- src/analyzers/ contains files from 5 zones (analyzers, analyzers-2, analyzers-3, cli, unit) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries [info]
- Audit the 6 specific src/analyzers/ production-file imports that cross into the unit/test layer (global finding 10) before reorganizing src/analyzers/ into subdirectories (global finding 8). If any target test utilities, fixtures, or unit-only types, extract those shared declarations to a neutral location such as src/types/ or src/shared/. This audit gate prevents the subdirectory reorganization from silently preserving a prod→test dependency under a cleaner directory structure. Recommended order: audit the 6 edges → extract shared types if needed → then proceed with subdirectory layout. [info]
- Recommended execution sequence for all actionable findings: (1) Verify and remove dead exports detectBranchLifecycle and resolveWorkedEpicTitlesForRange if confirmed unused. (2) Audit the 6 specific production→test import edges (global finding 5) and extract any shared declarations to src/types/ or src/shared/ if needed. (3) Decompose registerMcpTools in src/cli/mcp.ts into focused sub-registrar functions. (4) Apply subdirectory grouping within src/analyzers/ to collapse the three artificial Louvain zones into one coherent domain zone. Steps 1–3 are independent and can proceed in parallel; step 4 depends on step 2 being clean. [warning]
- Hub function: info in src/cli/output.ts is called from 9 files (high fan-in expected for this module type) — changes here have wide impact, consider if responsibilities can be narrowed [info]
- Potentially unused export: detectBranchLifecycle in src/analyzers/branch-work-filter.ts has no incoming calls — verify if still needed [info]
- ... and 3 more

## Tooling

This project uses n-dx for codebase analysis (Sourcevision) and requirement
tracking (Rex). MCP tools are available for interactive access to analysis
data and PRD management. Skills available: /plan, /status, /capture,
/zone, /work, /configure.
